stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] The nurse is caring for an 82-year-old client with bipolar disorder and heart failure who presents with new symptoms. Based on the client's electronic health record, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: This is the nurse for the 82-year-old client in room 302. At 07:00, the client was found to be acutely confused, only oriented to name and place.
Background: The client has a history of bipolar disorder, managed with lithium for 30 years, and new-onset heart failure diagnosed last month. They were started on hydrochlorothiazide and a 2-gram sodium diet.
Assessment: The client is lethargic with a coarse hand tremor and complains of tinnitus. Labs from this morning show a sodium of 129 mEq/L and a critical lithium level of 2.4 mEq/L.
Recommendation: I am holding the morning doses of lithium and HCTZ. I recommend an urgent provider evaluation for suspected lithium toxicity.

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
AssessmentResultReference Range
Serum Sodium (Na+)129 mEq/L135-145 mEq/L
Serum Lithium2.4 mEq/L0.6-1.2 mEq/L (Therapeutic)

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Lethargic, appears confused. Neuro: Oriented to name and place only. Coarse hand tremors noted bilaterally. Complains of tinnitus. No focal deficits. Cardio: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 auscultated. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Skin: Skin turgor is normal. No edema noted.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-Ray (Previous, one month ago): Mild cardiomegaly. No acute infiltrates or effusions noted.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Priority Nursing Diagnoses: 1. Risk for injury related to lithium toxicity. 2. Electrolyte imbalance related to hyponatremia. 3. Altered mental status related to lithium toxicity. Nursing Interventions: 1. Monitor serum lithium and sodium levels every 6 hours. 2. Assess neurological status every 2 hours. 3. Implement safety precautions to prevent falls. 4. Administer prescribed IV fluids as ordered. 5. Strict intake and output.

conditionOptions.rationale: While confusion can occur in older adults, the acute onset combined with specific neurological signs and lab values points away from a chronic condition. This is an acute toxic state.

Hyponatremia, caused by the low-sodium diet and HCTZ, leads to increased renal reabsorption of lithium. This causes the client's serum lithium level to rise to a toxic range, producing neurological symptoms.
The client's symptoms, such as coarse tremor and tinnitus, along with a lithium level of 2.4 mEq/L, are signs of severe toxicity, not expected or therapeutic side effects.

actionOptions.text: Hold next doses of Lithium and HCTZ
actionOptions.rationale: Holding both medications is the priority. Lithium is the source of the toxicity, and HCTZ is a primary contributing factor by causing sodium depletion, which must be stopped to prevent worsening.

actionOptions.text: Advise client to resume normal sodium diet
actionOptions.rationale: Resuming a normal sodium diet is essential to correct the underlying hyponatremia. This will help the kidneys excrete lithium more effectively and lower the toxic serum level.
Drinking large amounts of free water, especially distilled water, would further dilute serum sodium. This would worsen the hyponatremia and exacerbate lithium toxicity.
Increasing the HCTZ dose would cause further sodium and water loss. This action would worsen the hyponatremia and increase lithium retention, leading to more severe toxicity.

parameterOptions.text: Serum lithium, sodium, and renal function tests
parameterOptions.rationale: Monitoring lithium and sodium levels is essential to track the resolution of toxicity and electrolyte imbalance. Renal function tests (BUN/creatinine) are vital as lithium is cleared by the kidneys.
Assessing neurological status, tremors, and tinnitus provides a direct clinical measure of the client's response to treatment. Improvement in these signs indicates a reduction in lithium toxicity.
While HCTZ can affect glucose levels, it is not the priority in this acute toxic-metabolic emergency. The primary concern is neurotoxicity and electrolyte imbalance.

A positive Babinski sign indicates an upper motor neuron lesion. While severe neurotoxicity can cause various neurological signs, this is not a specific or primary parameter to monitor for lithium toxicity.

pearls.title: Early Recognition of Lithium Toxicity
pearls.description: Recognizing early signs of lithium toxicity, such as confusion, tremor, and tinnitus, is crucial for preventing severe complications.
pearls.strategy: Regularly assess clients on lithium for these symptoms, especially when changes in sodium intake or medications occur.

pearls.title: Impact of Sodium on Lithium Levels
pearls.description: Changes in sodium levels significantly affect lithium levels due to the kidneys' handling of both substances. Hyponatremia can lead to lithium retention and toxicity.
pearls.strategy: Educate clients on the importance of maintaining a consistent sodium intake and avoiding drastic dietary changes.

traps.title: Misinterpreting Symptoms as Normal Side Effects
traps.description: Early signs of lithium toxicity can be mistaken for normal side effects, delaying necessary interventions.
traps.strategy: Always consider lithium toxicity in clients presenting with new or worsening neurological symptoms, even if they are on a stable dose.

traps.title: Overlooking Drug Interactions
traps.description: Diuretics like HCTZ can significantly increase lithium levels, leading to toxicity if not monitored closely.
traps.strategy: Be vigilant about potential drug interactions, especially when starting or changing medications that affect sodium or fluid balance.

mnemonics.title: LITHIUM
mnemonics.description: A mnemonic to remember key aspects of lithium toxicity.
